name: Zen & the Dalai Lama
description: Modern Buddhist wisdom on mindfulness, compassion, and peace.
author: Quips Editorial

quotes.content: The purpose of our lives is to be happy.
quotes.notes: Opening premise of the Dalai Lama's dialogues with psychiatrist Howard Cutler.

quotes.content: If you want others to be happy, practice compassion. If you want to be happy, practice compassion.
quotes.notes: A recurring teaching on compassion as the root of one's own wellbeing.

quotes.content: Love and compassion are necessities, not luxuries. Without them, humanity cannot survive.
quotes.notes: The Dalai Lama frames compassion as essential to human survival, not a spiritual extra.

quotes.content: If you can, help others; if you cannot do that, at least do not harm them.
quotes.notes: The Dalai Lama's minimal ethic drawn from Buddhist non-harming.

quotes.content: The very purpose of religion is to control yourself, not to criticize others.
quotes.notes: From the Dalai Lama's argument for a secular, self-directed ethics.

quotes.content: It is not enough to be compassionate. You must act.
quotes.notes: The Dalai Lama insists that compassion be expressed in conduct, not sentiment alone.

quotes.content: The more you are motivated by love, the more fearless and free your action will be.
quotes.notes: From the Dalai Lama's week of dialogues with Archbishop Desmond Tutu.

quotes.content: The ultimate source of happiness is within us.
quotes.notes: The Dalai Lama locates lasting joy in the mind rather than in circumstances.

quotes.content: A lack of transparency results in distrust and a deep sense of insecurity.
quotes.notes: The Dalai Lama on honesty as the ground of trust and ease.

quotes.content: Feel free to become a beacon of compassion, wherever you are.
quotes.notes: The Dalai Lama's practical handbook on cultivating compassion in daily life.

quotes.content: Drink your tea slowly and reverently, as if it is the axis on which the world earth revolves.
quotes.notes: Thich Nhat Hanh's illustration of doing ordinary tasks with full attention.

quotes.content: While washing the dishes one should only be washing the dishes.
quotes.notes: The famous dishwashing example of staying present in the current moment.

quotes.content: The best way to take care of the future is to take care of the present moment.
quotes.notes: Thich Nhat Hanh on how presence, not planning, secures what comes next.

quotes.content: Peace is present right here and now, in ourselves and in everything we do and see.
quotes.notes: The keynote of Thich Nhat Hanh's most widely read book on everyday mindfulness.

quotes.content: Walk as if you are kissing the Earth with your feet.
quotes.notes: Thich Nhat Hanh's instruction for walking meditation.

quotes.content: Because you are alive, everything is possible.
quotes.notes: Thich Nhat Hanh on gratitude for the fact of being alive.

quotes.content: The most precious gift we can offer others is our presence.
quotes.notes: Thich Nhat Hanh on attention as the deepest form of love.

quotes.content: Life is available only in the present moment.
quotes.notes: A distilled statement of Thich Nhat Hanh's core teaching on presence.

quotes.content: To be beautiful means to be yourself. You don't need to be accepted by others. You need to accept yourself.
quotes.notes: Thich Nhat Hanh on self-acceptance as the source of true beauty.

quotes.content: If we are peaceful, if we are happy, we can smile, and everyone in our family, our entire society, will benefit from our peace.
quotes.notes: The title teaching of Being Peace, on inner peace radiating outward.

quotes.content: No mud, no lotus.
quotes.notes: Thich Nhat Hanh's image for suffering as the ground of happiness.

quotes.content: The art of happiness is also the art of suffering well.
quotes.notes: Thich Nhat Hanh on learning to handle pain skillfully rather than avoid it.

quotes.content: In the beginner's mind there are many possibilities, but in the expert's mind there are few.
quotes.notes: The book's most quoted line, defining shoshin, or beginner's mind.

quotes.content: Each of you is perfect the way you are, and you can use a little improvement.
quotes.notes: Suzuki Roshi's paradoxical encouragement to his students.

quotes.content: The most important point is to accept yourself and stand on your two feet.
quotes.notes: Suzuki on self-reliance in Zen practice.

quotes.content: When you do something, you should burn yourself completely, like a good bonfire, leaving no trace of yourself.
quotes.notes: Suzuki on wholehearted, complete engagement in each activity.

quotes.content: To give your sheep or cow a large, spacious meadow is the way to control him.
quotes.notes: Suzuki's metaphor for guiding the mind through spaciousness rather than force.

quotes.content: In the beginner's mind there is no thought, 'I have attained something.'
quotes.notes: Suzuki on the egolessness of true practice.

quotes.content: Zen is not some kind of excitement, but concentration on our usual everyday routine.
quotes.notes: Suzuki grounds Zen in ordinary life rather than dramatic experience.

quotes.content: Zen has no other special doctrine than to think, or to be in the state you are.
quotes.notes: The scholar who introduced Zen to the West, on its plainness.

quotes.content: The ego-shell in which we live is the hardest thing to outgrow.
quotes.notes: D.T. Suzuki on the self as the central obstacle in Zen.
